verses.commentary: He participates in Israel's worship before transforming it.
Near the Sheep Gate a pool called Bethesda surrounded by five colonnades. Specific geography. A place of desperate hope. The sick gather where healing is rumored.
Here a great number of disabled people lay: the blind, lame, paralyzed. Human misery concentrated. Suffering seeks any hope. Desperation brings people to dubious remedies.
Absent in earliest manuscripts. Later addition explaining popular belief about the pool's miraculous waters. Superstition fills the void of true healing.
One man had been an invalid for thirty-eight years. Chronic suffering. Nearly a lifetime of disability. Hope deferred makes the heart sick.
Jesus saw him, learned he had been in this condition a long time. Divine initiative. Jesus knows the man's history. Do you want to get well? Strange question with profound implications.

Sir, I have no one to help me into the pool. While I am trying, someone else goes down ahead of me. Excuse or explanation? He describes his helplessness. Competition for healing. Grace will bypass the system.
Get up! Pick up your mat and walk. Three commands. Instant demand for obedience. Jesus gives what He commands. The word creates the ability.
At once the man was cured. He picked up his mat and walked. The day was a Sabbath. Instant healing. Immediate obedience. But timing creates conflict. Grace clashes with religious rules.
The Jewish leaders said: It is the Sabbath; the law forbids you to carry your mat. They see violation, not miracle. Legalism blinds to mercy. Sabbath regulation trumps human restoration.
The man who made me well said, Pick up your mat and walk. He defers to higher authority. The healer's command outweighs religious leaders' prohibition. Power validates authority.
Who is this fellow who told you to pick it up and walk? They want to identify the troublemaker. Focus on violation, not healing. Missing the forest for the trees.
The man had no idea who it was, for Jesus had slipped away into the crowd. Anonymous grace. Jesus does not seek credit. The healer vanishes; the healing remains.

Later Jesus found him at the temple. See, you are well again. Stop sinning or something worse may happen. Connection between sin and suffering hinted but not explained. Warning suggests moral dimension to illness. Greater judgment looms beyond physical affliction.
The man went away and told the Jewish leaders it was Jesus. Informant or innocent reporter? Gratitude or betrayal? Ambiguous motive. The identification sets conflict in motion.
Because Jesus was doing these things on the Sabbath, the Jewish leaders began to persecute Him. Healing as crime. Mercy as offense. Religious system opposes the Messiah it was designed to recognize.

My Father is always at His work to this very day, and I too am working. Jesus defends Sabbath healing by claiming God's ongoing sustaining work. The Father does not cease maintaining creation on Sabbath.

They tried all the more to kill Him. Not only breaking the Sabbath but calling God His own Father, making Himself equal with God. They understand His claim correctly. Jesus asserts deity. They choose murder over worship.

The Son can do nothing by Himself; He can do only what He sees His Father doing. Whatever the Father does the Son also does. Perfect unity of will and action. Not independence but total alignment. Divine equality expressed through submission.
The Father loves the Son and shows Him all He does. He will show Him even greater works. Love within the Trinity. Full revelation to the Son. Greater works coming.
The Father raises the dead and gives them life. The Son gives life to whom He is pleased to give it. Resurrection power shared. Sovereignty in life-giving. Jesus has authority over death.
The Father judges no one but has entrusted all judgment to the Son. Total delegation of judicial authority. The Son will judge humanity. Future judgment assigned to Jesus.
That all may honor the Son just as they honor the Father. Equal honor. Whoever does not honor the Son does not honor the Father. Rejection of Jesus is rejection of God. No middle ground.

Whoever hears My word and believes Him who sent Me has eternal life and will not be judged but has crossed over from death to life. Present possession. Already secured. Verdict rendered now. Faith transfers from death to life.

A time is coming and has now come when the dead will hear the voice of the Son of God and those who hear will live. Spiritual resurrection now. The spiritually dead hear and live. Present salvation, not just future.
As the Father has life in Himself, so He has granted the Son also to have life in Himself. Self-existent life. Not derived but inherent. Divine attribute shared. The Son is the source of life.
He has given Him authority to judge because He is the Son of Man. Human and divine. The incarnate One judges humanity. Shared experience qualifies Him as judge.
Do not be amazed at this, for a time is coming when all in their graves will hear His voice. Future bodily resurrection. Universal scope. Jesus' voice summons the dead.
Those who have done good will rise to live; those who have done evil will rise to be condemned. Resurrection to judgment. Two destinies. Works reveal faith. Final separation.
Just judgment flows from submission to the Father. No self-interest distorts His verdict.
Self-testimony requires corroboration. Jesus appeals to external witnesses.
Divine testimony validates Jesus' claims.
Human testimony confirmed divine truth.
But He offers evidence to persuade. Mercy motivates His argument.
Fickle crowds. They liked John's message until it demanded response.

I have testimony weightier than John's. The works the Father has given Me to finish testify that the Father has sent Me. Works validate identity. Miracles are divine signatures. Completed mission confirms calling.
The Father who sent Me has Himself testified concerning Me. You have never heard His voice nor seen His form. Divine testimony given but not received. They claim to know God but miss His voice.
If they knew God, they would recognize Jesus.
Scripture points to Christ; they miss the referent. Knowledge without revelation.
They study the map but refuse the journey. Knowledge does not guarantee response. Stubbornness resists clear evidence.
He seeks the Father's approval alone. Human praise is irrelevant to His mission.
Lack of love for God explains rejection of Jesus. The heart reveals its allegiance.
Pride prefers self-made leaders over God's chosen.
Peer approval replaces divine approval. Social dynamics prevent faith.
Moses, their hero, condemns them. The Law they claim to follow exposes their failure. The one they revere becomes their accuser.
Deuteronomy 18:15-18. Pentateuch points to Christ. Rejecting Jesus is rejecting Moses.
If Scripture is rejected, Jesus' words will be too. Consistency in rejection.
